note:
Facebook表示，如果监管机构不让步，它将停止在欧洲的运营。
Facebook威胁称，如果欧洲监管机构不让步，让这家社交网络为所欲为，他们将收拾好自己的玩具回家。
在都柏林的一份法庭文件中，Facebook表示，爱尔兰数据保护委员会(DPC)的一项决定将迫使该公司撤资，让使用Facebook和照片分享服务Instagram的4.1亿人陷入困境。
[…]。Facebook所指的决定是上个月发布的一项初步命令，出于对美国政府监控数据的担忧，该命令要求停止将欧洲客户的数据传输到美国的服务器。
…。这应该是一种威胁吗？因为对我来说这听起来更像是礼物。求求你，扎克，回家吧！我想我们在欧洲没有你的犯罪活动也会过得很好。
note_en:
Home >   Legal >   Facebook says it will stop operating in Europe if regulators don’t back down
    Facebook has threatened to pack up its toys and go home if European regulators don’t back down and let the social network get its own way.
 In a court filing in Dublin, Facebook said that a decision by Ireland’s Data Protection Commission (DPC) would force the company to pull up stakes and leave the 410 million people who use Facebook and photo-sharing service Instagram in the lurch.
[…] The decision Facebook’s referring to is  a preliminary order handed down last month to stop the transfer of data about European customers to servers in the U.S., over concerns about U.S. government surveillance of the data.
 …is this supposed to be a threat? Because it sounds more like a gift to me. Please, Zuck, go home! I think we here in Europe will do just fine without your criminal enterprise.
title: Facebook表示，如果监管机构不让步，它将停止在欧洲的运营
title_en: Facebook says it will stop operating in Europe if regulators don’t back down

note:
设计和生产一辆新车需要大量的时间和金钱，所以汽车销售的时间越长，收回投资的时间就越多。八年是关于一款新车型的寿命，不幸的是，这段时间足以让客户感到厌倦。
进入中年刷新。这个想法听起来很简单--在一款车型的使用寿命过半时，它会被改头换面。总有一些东西可以调整，让生产变得更容易，让客户满意，或者利用同时出现的一些新的小玩意儿或小玩意儿。
保时捷(Porsche&39；)的Panamera轿车就是这样的例子，这款车刚从造型部门脱颖而出，拥有更锐利的新面孔，对尾灯进行了微妙的调整，我们的观众最感兴趣的是，车型阵容中的插电式混合动力车的电池尺寸增加了27%。
在过去，保时捷会在一个地方组装精选的整容轿车，让我们记者每辆都有几个小时的时间。在我们新的社交距离噩梦中，该公司采取了另一种方式，将一辆2021年规格的Panamera 4S电动混合动力车带给我在华盛顿呆了几天。它实际上是一个新增加的插电式Panamera阵容，插槽之间的Panamera 4 E混合和Panamera涡轮S E混合。
大约90马力(或67kw)，由增强的2.9L双涡轮增压V6发动机提供434hp(323kW)的4S。电动马达仍然与其他保时捷混合动力车一样，134hp(100kW)，295lb-ft(400 Nm)，比如我们昨天回顾的卡宴(Cayenne)。电机再次位于内燃机和驱动所有四个轮子的八速自动ZF变速器之间。
因此，综合功率和扭矩是552马力(412kW)和553磅英尺(750 Nm)，这在一定程度上解释了为什么保时捷决定放弃非混合动力的Panamera Turbo-也有约550马力-从阵容中。
2021年的另一个变化，也适用于另外两款插电式Panamera，是更大的锂离子电池。它现在是17.9千瓦时，高于之前的14.1千瓦时，并且按照新能源部署和再生战略进行了编程，这些战略应该会转化为现实世界中燃油效率的提高，以及纯电动范围的有意义的提升。改进后的电池意味着新电池不会占用比出厂电池更多的空间。
EPA的官方数据要到明年年初新款Panamera上市时才能确定，但我的测试车270英里的平均行驶里程为23.4英里(101英里/100公里)。
一辆欧洲规格的汽车(因此公共充电器的接头错误)和我家停车位旁边没有插座的组合让事情变得复杂起来。但是在Sports Plus模式下30分钟，18英里(30公里)的车程将电池带到了50%的充电状态，这足以让我在不启动内燃机的情况下再次回家。(另一个调整是，在Sports Plus或E-Charge模式下，这两种模式都使用V6作为发电机为电池充电，只会使4S达到80%的SoC，而不是100%。)。
尽管可以使用超过550马力和3.5秒的0-60英里时速，但Panamera 4S电动混合动力车感觉并不是一辆特别快的车。当你启动方向盘输入或将脚从静止状态放在油门上时，你会注意到这一点。
当你让它做点什么的时候，这辆车不会有太多犹豫，但它感觉起来很重，就像它是用钨锻造的，而不是高强度钢和铝的混合物。这是我开过的所有Panamera的一个共同特点，而同样笨重的卡宴(Cayenne)或泰康(Taycan)则不具备这一特点，这让我得出结论，Panamera的客户喜欢这样。
与EPA能效评级一样，定价也要到2021年第一季度才会公布，届时Panamera将于2021年开始抵达美国，面向客户。所以我不能告诉你你选择了便宜的Panamera 4电动混合动力车比4S电动混合动力车节省了多少钱。这将是我的选择，也许是用节省下来的钱增加一些选择。(如果你需要插电式混合动力保时捷，我会推荐卡宴(Cayenne)SUV；如果你能忍受电池电动车，我会推荐泰坎(Taycan)。)
note_en:
It takes a lot of time and money to design and then produce a new vehicle, so the longer the car stays on sale, the more time there is to recoup that investment. Eight years is about the lifespan of a new model, which unfortunately is long enough for customers to get bored.
 Enter the midlife refresh. The idea is as simple as it sounds—halfway through a model's lifespan, it gets treated to a makeover. There are always things that can be tweaked to make production easier, to satisfy customers, or to take advantage of some new gadget or gizmo that showed up in the meantime.
 Such is the case for Porsche's Panamera sedan, which emerges fresh from the styling department with a sharper new face, a subtle tweak to the taillights, and of most interest to our audience, a 27 percent bump in battery size for the plug-in hybrids in the model lineup.
 In days of old, Porsche would have assembled a selection of its face-lifted sedans in one spot and let us journalists have a couple of hours in each. In our new, socially distanced nightmare, the company has taken another approach, bringing a 2021-spec Panamera 4S E-Hybrid to me in DC for a couple of days instead. It's actually a new addition to the plug-in Panamera lineup, slotting in between the  Panamera 4 E-Hybrid and the  Panamera Turbo S E-Hybrid.
  About 90hp (or 67kW), courtesy of an uprated 2.9L twin-turbo V6 which offers 434hp (323kW) in the 4S. The electric motor remains the same 134hp (100kW), 295lb-ft (400Nm) as in other Porsche hybrids, like the Cayenne we reviewed yesterday. The motor is again situated between the internal combustion engine and the eight-speed automatic ZF transmission, which drives all four wheels.
 Thus, combined power and torque is a hefty 552hp (412kW) and 553lb-ft (750Nm), which goes some way to explaining why Porsche has decided to drop the non-hybrid Panamera Turbo—also with about 550hp—from the lineup.
 The other change for 2021, which also applies to the other two plug-in Panameras, is a bigger lithium-ion battery. It's now 17.9kWh, up from 14.1kWh, and arrives programmed with new energy deployment and regeneration strategies that should translate to a real-world increase in fuel efficiency, as well as a meaningful bump in its electric-only range. Improved cells mean that the new battery doesn't occupy any more room than the outgoing unit.
 The official EPA numbers won't be determined until closer early next year when the new Panameras go on sale, but my test car averaged 23.4mpg (10l/100km) over 270 miles.
 The combination of a Euro-spec car (therefore the wrong connector for public chargers) and no outlet by my parking space at home complicated things. But a 30-minute, 18-mile (30km) drive in Sport Plus mode brought the battery to a 50 percent state of charge, which was enough energy to get me back home again without firing up the internal combustion engine. (Another tweak is that in Sport Plus or E-Charge mode, both of which use the V6 as a generator to charge the battery, will only bring the 4S to 80 percent SoC instead of 100 percent.)
  Despite more than 550hp to play with and 3.5-second 0-60mph time, the Panamera 4S E-Hybrid doesn't feel like a particularly fast car. You notice it when you initiate a steering input or apply your foot to the accelerator from a standstill.
 The car doesn't so much hesitate when you tell it to do something, but it feels heavy, like it's forged from tungsten rather than a mix of high-strength steel and aluminum. This is a common trait among all the Panameras I've driven,  one not shared by the similarly heavy Cayenne or  Taycan, which leads me to conclude that Panamera customers like it like that.
 Pricing, like EPA efficiency ratings, won't be announced until Q1 2021, when 2021 Panameras start arriving in the United States for customers. So I can't tell you how much you'd save by picking the cheaper Panamera 4 E-Hybrid over the 4S E-Hybrid. That would be my pick, perhaps with the savings spent adding some options. (I'd actually recommend the Cayenne SUV if you need a plug-in hybrid Porsche, and the Taycan if you can live with battery electric vehicle.)
title: 保时捷将2021年Panamera混合动力车的电池容量提高27%
title_en: Porsche boosts battery capacity by 27 percent for 2021 Panamera hybrid

note:
梦想有一天殖民火星的太空爱好者必须面对这个星球有限的自然资源的严峻现实，特别是在建筑材料方面。新加坡科技大学(Singapore University Of Technology And Design)的一组科学家发现，利用简单的化学手段，昆虫和甲壳类动物外骨骼中含有的有机聚合物甲壳素可以很容易地转化为一种可行的建筑材料，用于制作基本工具和栖息地。这将需要最少的能量，并且不需要运输专门的设备。科学家们在最近发表在“公共科学图书馆·综合”杂志上的一篇论文中描述了他们的实验。
合著者哈维尔·费尔南德斯(Javier Fernandez)说，这项技术最初是为了在城市环境中创建循环生态系统而开发的。但由于它的效率，它也是在一个没有生命的星球或卫星的极其稀缺的环境中，在封闭的人工生态系统中生产材料的最有效和最可扩展的方法。
正如我们之前报道的那样，NASA宣布了一项雄心勃勃的计划，将美国宇航员送回月球，并在那里建立一个永久基地，着眼于最终将宇航员送上火星。材料科学将是阿耳特弥斯登月计划成功的关键，特别是当涉及到建造一个可行的月球(或火星)基地所需的材料时。例如，混凝土需要大量的附加水才能在原地使用，而月球和火星上的水都明显短缺。运输成本将高得令人望而却步。美国国家航空航天局(NASA)估计，仅将一磅材料送入轨道的成本约为1万美元。
因此，利用月球上现有的材料建造月球基地的可能性受到了极大的关注。过去的提案要求使用Sorel水泥进行3D打印，这需要大量的化学品和水(消耗品)，以及一种岩石状的材料，需要水和磷酸作为液体粘合剂。早在3月份，一个国际科学家团队发表的一篇论文建议，在月球上建立基地的宇航员可以使用尿液中的尿素作为塑化剂，从月壤中创造出一种混凝土般的建筑材料。
与月球一样，任何在火星上建立宜居基地的计划都必须采用利用这颗红色星球的浮冰的制造技术。但这篇论文的作者指出，大多数符合要求的陆地制造战略通常需要专门的设备和大量的能源。然而，作者们写道，大自然提出了生命适应恶劣环境的成功策略。在生物有机体中，刚性结构是通过以较低的能量成本(例如，碳酸钙)集成来自环境的无机填料并结合到以相对较高的代谢成本产生的有机基质(例如，甲壳素)中形成的。
费尔南德斯和他的同事们坚持认为，甲壳素很可能是任何计划中的人工生态系统的一部分，因为它在自然界中是如此丰富。例如，它是鱼鳞和真菌细胞壁的主要成分，也是甲壳类和昆虫的外骨骼。事实上，昆虫已经成为可能的火星基地的关键蛋白质来源。作者写道，由于昆虫中的甲壳素成分对人类的营养价值有限，提取它来制造建筑材料并不会阻碍食品供应，也不会与食品供应竞争。更确切地说，它是它的副产品。
在他们的实验中，研究人员依赖于相当简单的化学。他们提取了从虾中提取的壳聚糖，将其溶解在乙酸中-这是有氧和厌氧发酵的常见副产品-并将其与一种相当于火星土壤的矿物结合起来，形成了他们的几丁质建筑材料。他们通过用它制作各种物体来测试它的性能，其中最著名的是一个功能扳手，他们通过拧紧一个六角形螺栓进行了测试。虽然承认这不太可能在某些关键的空间应用中取代金属工具，但事实证明，它足够坚韧，足以维持足够的扭矩来完成日常小任务。
接下来，研究小组尝试将这种材料塑造成各种几何形状，以通过添加制造来研究其作为建筑材料的潜力，从圆柱体和立方体到既有圆形又有棱角的物体-包括一个小小的类人火星人物。科学家们还证明，这种生物石块可以用作临时砂浆，有效地堵住管道上的一个小孔。管道随后几个星期没有漏水。最后，他们建立了一个完整的3D打印模型，其中一个可能的火星栖息地设计，花了不到两个小时就完成了。研究人员得出结论，他们的结果证明了这种闭环的可行性，
费尔南德斯说，生物灵感制造和可持续材料不是合成聚合物的替代技术，而是一种使能技术，定义了制造中的新范式，并允许做合成同行无法实现的事情。我们已经证明，它们不仅是我们在地球上可持续发展的关键，也是人类下一个最大成就之一的关键：我们向星际物种的转变。
title: 研究发现，甲壳素可以用来在火星上建造工具和栖息地
title_en: Chitin could be used to build tools and habitats on Mars, study finds

note:
这些努力与全国选民登记日保持一致，这一天突显了Twitter、Facebook和Snapchat等公司正在进行的让人们参加投票的努力，无论是IRL还是通过邮寄投票。
Snapchat表示，它已经帮助75万美国用户注册投票，该公司宣布了新的以选民为重点的节目，名人名单不拘一格，其中包括斯努普·道格(Snoop Dogg)、前总统奥巴马和阿诺德·施瓦辛格(Arnold Schwarzenegger)。这家社交网络还推出了一些新的Snap Originals(该公司的简短内容)，主题是美国大选。其中包括一张“祝你好运的投票人！”彼得·汉比(Peter Hamby)的迷你剧，是“当黑人与MK·阿桑特在一起”(While Black With MK Asante)中以选举为。
撇开明星影响力不谈，Snapchat工具可以提示用户制定与朋友一起投票的计划，还允许他们通过应用程序本身请求邮寄投票。Snapchat发现页面上的任何账户都可以直接将该平台的选民登记工具嵌入到他们的内容中。
推特自己的努力也把选民登记放在了最重要的位置。9月22日，所有美国Twitter用户都收到了一个提示，要求他们通过TurboVote登记或确认他们的选民登记。提示和随之而来的推送警报以40种语言发送。新的提醒加入了该公司现有的投票信息中心和#YourVoiceYourVote活动，该活动招募了Marshmello和Chrissy Teigen等热门账户，以推广Vote.org注册检查页面的链接以及原始语音笔记。推特还增加了与#NationalVoterRegistrationDay和#VoteReady链接的新标签表情符号。
除了现有的努力，Facebook还推出了自己的选举信息中心、头条新闻提示和Instagram提醒，还邀请了名人艾丽西亚·凯斯(Alicia Keys)、加布里埃尔·尤恩(Gabrielle Union)、贾达·平克特·史密斯(Jada Pinkett Smith)和其他人一起围绕选民登记举办了一系列特别的“Vote-A-Thon 2020”直播教育公益广告。Facebook表示，它已经帮助250万人在Facebook、Instagram和Messenger上注册投票。
Facebook提醒用户注册的首页通知将在Facebook应用程序、Instagram和Messenger上运行到9月25日(周五)。在全国选民登记日使用一套新的Instagram贴纸的用户，他们的故事将在社交网络上的一个特别的选民登记主题故事中亮相。
YouTube和显然仍处于不确定状态的TikTok周二似乎没有开展自己的选民登记活动，但谷歌、Reddit和不和谐都以醒目的主页横幅提醒用户登记投票。
note_en:
The efforts align with  National Voter Registration Day, which punctuates ongoing efforts from companies like Twitter, Facebook and Snapchat to get people to the polls, whether IRL or through mail-in voting.
 Snapchat, which says it has helped 750,000 U.S. users get registered to vote, announced new voter-focused programming with an  eclectic slate of celebrities that included Snoop Dogg, former President Obama and Arnold Schwarzenegger. The social network is also launching some new Snap Originals (the company’s short form pieces of content) themed around the U.S. election. Those include a “Good Luck Voter!” miniseries by Peter Hamby a special election-focused episode of “While Black with MK Asante.”
  Star power aside, a Snapchat tool prompts users make a plan to vote with friends and also allows them to request a mail-in ballot through the app itself. Any account featured on Snapchat’s Discover page can also embed the platform’s voter registration tool directly into their content.
  Twitter’s   own efforts also put voter registration front and center. On September 22, all U.S. Twitter users were sent a prompt asking them to register or confirm their voter registration through TurboVote. The prompt and an accompanying push alert were sent in 40 languages. The new reminders join the company’s existing voting info hub and its  #YourVoiceYourVote campaign, which recruited popular accounts like Marshmello and Chrissy Teigen to promote a link to Vote.org’s registration check page along with original voice notes. Twitter also added new hashtag emoji linked to #NationalVoterRegistrationDay and #VoteReady.
   Adding to its  existing efforts, like the launch of its own election info hub, top-of-newsfeed prompts and Instagram reminders, Facebook roped in celebrities Alicia Keys, Gabrielle Union, Jada Pinkett Smith and others for a special “Vote-A-Thon 2020” series of  livestreaming educational PSAs around voter registration. Facebook says it’s helped 2.5 million people get registered to vote across  Facebook,   Instagram and Messenger
  Facebook’s top-of-feed notifications reminding users to register will run on the Facebook app, Instagram and Messenger through Friday September 25. Users who use a new set of Instagram stickers for National Voter Registration Day will have their story featured in a special voter registration-themed story on the social network.
 YouTube and the apparently  still-in-limbo TikTok didn’t appear to be holding their own voter registration drives Tuesday, but Google, Reddit and Discord all featured prominent homepage banners reminding users to register to vote.
title: 本周，社交网络正在进行一场选民登记闪电战。
title_en: Social networks are doing a voter registration blitz this week

Mirakl为其市场平台筹集了3亿美元-Mirakl帮助公司在其电子商务网站上推出和管理市场。
Pure Shicraft以2300万美元的A系列提升其电动舷外马达-Pure Shicraft正在建造一种电动舷外马达，可以取代普通的燃气马达，以满足大多数船艇需求。
尽管数字媒体经历了艰难的一年，但Blativity和Shade Room正在蓬勃发展-回顾了我与Blativity的Morgan DeBaun和Shade Room的Angelica Nwandu的中断讨论。
大型科技公司的房间里有两头大象：隐私和竞争-显然，即使是老牌的科技公司也会紧张地讨论这个话题。
海盗船游戏公司(Corsair Gaming)是如何公布如此令人印象深刻的IPO前数据的？-该公司成立于1994年，与其说是一家初创公司，不如说是一家成熟的企业。
(提醒：Extra Crunch是我们的订阅会员计划，旨在使初创企业的信息大众化。您可以在这里注册。)。
TikTok，微信与中美之间不断扩大的数字鸿沟-凯瑟琳·舒讨论了两国科技公司之间关系的戏剧性转变。
科技公司必须从根本上重新考虑如何对待独立承包商-就像新冠肺炎加快了远程工作的步伐一样，我们目前的危机加速了雇用独立承包商的趋势。
Bose推出了一对新的睡眠耳塞-Sleepbuds II的推出时机再好不过了。
The Daily Crunch是TechCrunch对我们最重大、最重要的故事的综合报道。如果你想在太平洋时间每天下午3点左右把它送到你的收件箱，你可以在这里订阅。
note_en:
Microsoft announced today that it’s ready to compete with Twilio by  launching a set of features that allow developers to add voice and video calling, chat, text messages and old-school telephony to their apps.
  “Azure Communication Services is built natively on top a global, reliable cloud — Azure,” wrote Microsoft’s Scott Van Vliet. “Businesses can confidently build and deploy on the same low latency global communication network used by Microsoft Teams to support 5B+ meeting minutes daily.”
 This is just one of a number of announcements that Microsoft made at  its Ignite conference this morning. Other additions include a platform for  detecting biological threats and the  Azure Orbital service for satellite operators.
  Google launches a work-tracking tool and Airtable rival, Tables — Tables’ bots help users do things like scheduling recurring email reminders when tasks are overdue and messaging a chat room when new form submissions are received.
 Amazon adds support for Kannada, Malayalam, Tamil and Telugu in local Indian languages push ahead of Diwali — The company said this move should help it reach an additional 200-300 million users in India.
 Pinterest breaks daily download record due to user interest in iOS 14 design ideas — Following the release of iOS 14, the excitement around the ability to customize your iPhone home screen has been paying off for Pinterest.
  Mirakl raises $300 million for its marketplace platform — Mirakl helps companies launch and manage a marketplace on their e-commerce websites.
 Pure Watercraft ramps up its electric outboard motors with a $23 million series A — Pure Watercraft is building an electric outboard motor that can replace a normal gas one for most boating needs.
   Despite a rough year for digital media, Blavity and The Shade Room are thriving — A recap of my Disrupt discussion with Morgan DeBaun of Blavity and Angelica Nwandu of The Shade Room.
 Big tech has 2 elephants in the room: Privacy and competition — There’s clearly a nervousness among even well-established tech firms to discuss this topic.
 How has Corsair Gaming posted such impressive pre-IPO numbers? — The company was founded in 1994, making it more of a mature business than a startup.
 (Reminder: Extra Crunch is our subscription membership program, which aims to democratize information about startups.  You can sign up here.)
  TikTok, WeChat and the growing digital divide between the US and China — Catherine Shu discusses the dramatic shift in the relationship between tech companies in both countries.
 Tech must radically rethink how it treats independent contractors — Just as COVID-19 has accelerated the move to remote work, our current crisis has accelerated the trend toward hiring independent contractors.
 Bose introduces a new pair of sleep-focused earbuds — The timing of the Sleepbuds II could hardly be better.

为什么Spotify有这么多像“白噪音婴儿睡眠”这样的怪诞、通俗的艺人？
这个平台充斥着搜索优化的垃圾邮件发送者，而且看不到尽头。
欢迎收看Spotify诈骗者指南，这是一个关于Spotify的骗局，讲述了在这个流行的流媒体服务上积累流媒体、赚钱和声名狼藉的阴谋。
你可能从未听说过他们，但放松音乐疗法在音乐生涯中取得了相当大的成功。至少在Spotify上是这样。
这位“艺人”在该平台上每月拥有超过50万听众，这一切都要归功于一个简单的诀窍：优化他们的名字，使其出现在Spotify的搜索结果中。
如果你不是Drake，这个平台每条流只支付三分之一便士，但它号称拥有超过25亿活跃用户。因此，如果你的音乐在搜索词中排名很高，你可以积累足够的听众，以最小的努力稳定地每月赚取数百美元，在某些情况下是数千美元。
成功的关键是找到一个Spotify用户可能会在搜索中键入的假艺人名字。就像放松音乐疗法一样，这些“艺术家”中的一些人使用的名字灵感来自于一个通常用来描述音乐的形容词。另一些人则根据某些音乐的流行用途来命名自己，比如众所周知的一般曲调，如儿童韵律，或者整个音乐流派。通常，这些创建者会进一步优化，用相关的单词给曲目和专辑加标题，然后即兴重新上传相同的歌曲，当过滤成只看到一首曲子时，这看起来特别荒谬。例如，放松音乐疗法，到目前为止，已经上传了616次“雨中森林里的溪流”这首曲目。
SEO Spam和它的各种Streambait表亲正好符合Spotify自己的营销战略，即“为任何情绪提供音乐”的一站式商店，而不是，比方说，一个突出最有才华的艺术家的中心。2017年，行业出版物音乐商业全球(Music Business Worldwide)透露，Spotify精心策划的播放列表中充斥着以特定平台的化名工作的艺术家，比如“查尔斯·博尔特”(Charles Bolt)，根本没有互联网下的存在。(该公司没有回应对这篇文章的多次置评请求。)
伯克利音乐学院(Berklee College Of Music)音乐商业和管理学副教授、战略咨询公司GHS的创始人乔治·霍华德(George Howard)非常清楚Spotify的SEO战略。“播放…。通过一系列的用户体验[决定]，他们成功地让绝大多数听众将音乐的创作者与创作的产品脱钩，“霍华德告诉OneZero。“企业家们所做的就是，‘好吧，我们会搜索引擎优化的。我们会从用户以及他或她如何搜索音乐的角度来考虑。‘“。
SubmitMusic创始人杰森·瑟夫(Jason Cerf)就是这些精明的上传者之一。他的公司从不同流派的个人制作人和艺术家那里购买音乐录音，然后将它们“定位为货币化的”，这一过程包括与客户合作，挑选适合流媒体的艺术家名字、曲目名称和专辑艺术。他并没有公然用万圣节派对恐怖声音和FX等令人惊讶的别名发布歌曲，但对于SubmitMusic的目录底线来说，类似的战术方法很重要，Cerf估计大约有1万首歌曲。
瑟夫告诉OneZero：“我们有一个创意团队，他们大规模开发元数据策略。”“我们有集思广益的会议，我们会想出一些艺术家的名字，这些名字可能会被认为是‘品牌’，或者，你可能会说，是通用的。”
这一策略如何体现取决于内容。“如果你给自己取名为‘乔·史密斯’，然后推出一张古典音乐专辑，它不会引起数字消费者的共鸣。如果你把古典音乐命名为“classque Elegante”，那就是放在…前面和中心位置的一条自有品牌的数据。而且很可能会有更多的数字活动，也就是点击量。“。
如果游戏搜索引擎优化的目标仅仅是将一些先前存在的音乐目录货币化，而不是真正建立追随者、举办演唱会或推动某一流派向前发展，那么游戏搜索引擎优化就特别有效。即使用户在无意中发现了这首歌后最终成为了它的粉丝，通常也没有办法在Spotify平台之外找到这位音乐家，因为艺术家的名字本身和随附的元数据只是SEO单词沙拉。
瑟夫说，他不会用具有误导性的方式给艺术家命名。他说，虽然Spotify没有针对SEO诱饵的成文规定，但他从数字分销商的联系人那里听说，Spotify通常不鼓励这种做法。虽然Spotify公司直接推荐的首选名单上的分销商更有可能采纳这些建议，但很明显，其他分销商在很大程度上并没有遵循这些建议。
要求谷歌播放“放松的音乐”，或者在Spotify的搜索栏中插入“冥想”，你会发现大量的艺术家账号，比如双耳节拍睡眠、自然之声自然音乐和空调声音，它们可以批量上传环境无人机、循环和弦进行或直截了当的白噪音。Spotify的用户群显然很难入睡，而巨大的受众兴趣使其成为潜在的SEO垃圾邮件发送者值得一试的竞争对手。与其他类型的音乐相比，普通的新时代音乐作品相对容易制作(或翻录)，这也不会有什么坏处。
因此，似乎没完没了的“艺术家”们在争夺各种与健康相关的音频的利基市场上展开了一场音频地盘之争。这些个人资料通常有几十到数百张专辑，每一张都充斥着关键字。使用这种方法的单个页面有规律地积累了数万到数十万的月度播放量。一些菊花链在他们的元数据中以艺术家为特色，以略有不同的名字填充多个页面，而另一些则只是在重新打包的专辑中一遍又一遍地重新上传完全相同的曲目。
另一个策略是流行的、通俗的、以儿童为中心的歌曲的基本版本，比如“生日快乐”(Happy Birthday)。事实上，仅“艺术家”“生日快乐”一家就上传了成百上千个相同的十几岁版本的曲子(从EDM到声学的不同风格)，每月有超过14万名听众。另一个账号，儿童音乐，通过一遍又一遍地分享拼写错误的经典歌曲，如“公交车上的车轮”和“如果你的快乐和你知道的”，每月有近30万听众。
一些“艺术家”的个人资料以整个音乐子流派命名，其中一些可能没有专门的流派页面，或者没有明确标记的Spotify创建的播放列表。“Lofi ChillHop”和“LoFi Chill”(都是经过认证的艺术家)似乎在争夺某种器乐的粉丝，这种音乐最近经历了一次重生，这在一定程度上要归功于一种模因，通过上传来历不明的节拍，每月分别有超过10万和4万名听众。经过认证的艺人个人资料“UK Drill”利用了人们对英国嘻哈音乐日益浓厚的兴趣，简单地翻录和转发了新兴MC的曲目，每月有超过8万名听众。账号“巫师之家”(一种恐怖的中期电子音乐风格)的幕后黑手通过上传通用乐器，并将流行的EDM歌曲、专辑和艺术家的名字塞进曲目的元数据中，积累了少量的流量。女巫之家的音乐本身甚至不是女巫之家，而是故意贴错标签的曲目，这些曲目是从晦涩难懂的器乐采样器专辑等地方窃取的。
Spotify并没有真正的动机去清理重复的曲目或古怪的艺人名字。在某种程度上，这些多产的海报只是在执行Spotify首席执行官丹尼尔·埃克(Daniel Ek)设想的一个极端版本，在这个世界里，艺术家们在音乐上花费的时间更少，而更注重音量。正如利兹·佩利(Liz Pelly)2017年在“障碍物”(The Baffler)中写道的那样，该平台自己的目标是(可以说)“让音乐舒适地融入到一个情感调节胶囊中，这个胶囊经过优化，可以获得最大的点击量。”
例如，瑟夫认为他正在做的事情是一个有用的实用工具。他说：“人们希望不仅能按艺人名字搜索音乐，还能按关键字搜索。”“他们想说，‘哦，我在睡觉，或者我在开车，或者我在冥想，或者我有个孩子，他需要睡觉，或者我只是需要背景音乐。’”
事实上，Spotify算法的一个基本组成部分是简单地识别听众何时成功地找到了他们正在寻找的东西，而不是识别音乐本身是否“高质量”。正如2017年秃鹫的一篇文章所指出的那样，这些艺术家“正在为人们提供他们想要的东西。无独有偶，他们想要的只是昙花一现的废话。“。
尽管如此，一些这样的音乐是彻头彻尾的欺骗性的，要么是明显的低质量，要么是与给定的艺术家头衔几乎没有联系。
数字音乐分销商和Spotify合作伙伴DistroKid的DistroLock音频指纹技术，以及Spotify自己的区块链初创公司收购等工具，理论上可以通过限制相同曲目的重传来遏制一些垃圾邮件。但这类支票还没有进入Spotify的平台。
增加Spotify图书馆的人性化程度可以提供另一种解决方案，但这带来了一个后勤问题，因为该平台的目录范围太广。(该平台表示，它每天都会向目录中添加数万首歌曲。)
Spotify的编辑团队对人们经常玩游戏的某些术语进行功能性干预，基本上采用了与垃圾邮件发送者相同的策略，为某些“流派和情绪”的音乐创建官方页面，每个页面都有在这些分类中精心挑选的播放列表。然而，目前还不清楚Spotify编辑团队中的谁制作这些页面，他们使用什么过程来创建这些页面，以及他们的明确目的是否是为了摆脱垃圾邮件。这些Spotify官方页面和播放列表有时在搜索量上排名靠前，但它们往往面临着来自艺人的大量竞争，这些艺人的名字几乎是一组高价值术语的所有可以想象到的变体。此外，垃圾邮件发送者自己偶尔也会出现在批准的播放列表中，经常会有大量侵扰性较小的假艺人加入。
霍华德并没有因为人们在这个平台上玩游戏来让他们的音乐被听到而感到不满，他强调说，使用这些策略的人只是在一个破碎的系统中工作。
“这项技术是不可知的，”他说。“不使用(Spotify的)系统对你有利是愚蠢的，所以人们只是在玩弄这个系统。我可以代表出版界精明的经营者说，‘我有所有这些内容。我将对其进行优化，以便于播放。他们做的是正确的事！“
note_en:
Why Spotify Has So Many Bizarre, Generic Artists Like ‘White Noise Baby Sleep’
 The platform is filled with search-optimized spammers, and there’s no end in sight
  Welcome to   The Cheater’s Guide to Spotify , a series about the schemes that rack up streams, money, and infamy on the popular streaming service.
 You’ve probably never heard of them, but  Relaxing Music Therapy  has had a pretty damn successful music career. At least, on Spotify.
 This “artist” has more than 500,000 monthly listeners on the platform, all thanks to One Simple Trick: optimizing their name to show up prominently in Spotify’s search results.
  While the platform pays only in the neighborhood of  a third of a penny per stream if  you’re not Drake, it  boasts more than a quarter-billion active users. So, if your music ranks highly for a search term, you can accumulate enough listens to steadily make hundreds, in some cases thousands, of dollars a month with minimal effort.
 The key to success is to find a phony artist name that Spotify users are likely to type into search. Like Relaxing Music Therapy, some of these “artists” use names inspired by an adjective commonly used to describe music. Others name themselves after popular uses for certain kinds of music, well-known generic tunes like children’s rhymes, or entire music genres. Often, these creators optimize further by titling tracks and albums with related words and reuploading the same songs ad nauseum, which can look especially absurd when filtering to see just a single tune. Relaxing Music Therapy, for instance, has uploaded the track “Stream in the Forest With Rain” 616 times to date.
  SEO spam and  its various streambait cousins fit right in  with Spotify’s own marketing strategy of being a one-stop shop for “music for any mood,” rather than, say, a hub that highlights the most talented artists. In 2017, the trade publication  Music Business Worldwide  revealed that Spotify’s curated playlists were filled with artists working under platform-specific pseudonyms, such as “Charles Bolt,” with no off-internet presence at all. (The company did not respond to multiple requests for comment on this article.)
 George Howard, an associate professor of music business and management at Berklee College of Music and founder of strategic consulting firm GHS, is well aware of the Spotify SEO strategy. “Spotify… has managed, through a whole series of UX [decisions], to make the vast majority of their listeners decouple the creator of the music from the product of that creation,” Howard told  OneZero. “What entrepreneurs have done is go, ‘Okay, we’ll SEO it. We’ll think in terms in terms of the user and how he or she is searching for music.’”
   SubmitMusic founder Jason Cerf is one of these shrewd uploaders. His company buys musical recordings from individual producers and artists across genres and then “positions them to be monetized,” a process that includes working with clients to pick streaming-friendly artist names, track names, and album art. He isn’t  quite putting out songs under blatantly eyebrow-raising aliases like  Halloween Party Scary Sounds and Fx, but a similarly tactical approach is important to the bottom line of SubmitMusic’s catalog, which Cerf estimates to be around 10,000 songs.
 “We have a creative team who develop at scale metadata strategies,” Cerf told  OneZero. “We have brainstorming sessions where we come up with artist names that will probably be recognized as ‘on brand’ or possibly, you could say, generic.”
 How this strategy manifests itself depends on the content. “If you name [yourself] ‘Joe Smith,’ and you put out a classical music album, it’s not going to resonate with a digital consumer. If you put out classical music under the name ‘Classique Elegante,’ that is an on-brand piece of data that is put up in front and center… and is likely going to harbor more digital activity, or clicks.”
 Gaming SEO is particularly effective if the goal is simply to monetize some preexisting catalog of music, without any aim to actually build a following, throw a concert, or push a genre forward. Even if a user ends up becoming a fan of the music after inadvertently discovering it, there’s often no way to find the musician outside of the Spotify platform, as the artists’ name itself and accompanying metadata are just SEO word salad.
  Cerf says he does not go so far as to name artists in ways that are misleading. While there’s no codified rule from Spotify against SEO bait, he says he’s heard from contacts at digital distributors that Spotify generally discourages the practice. Though distributors on Spotify’s  preferred list, which the company recommends directly, are more likely to follow these suggestions, it’s clear others aren’t, to a significant degree.
 Ask Google to play “relaxing music,” or plug “meditation” into Spotify’s search bar, and you’ll find heaps of artist accounts with names like  Binaural Beats Sleep,  Nature Sounds Nature Music, and  Air Conditioner Sound that mass-upload ambient drones, looped chord progressions, or straight-up white noise. Spotify’s user base apparently has a lot of trouble sleeping, and that significant audience interest makes it a worthwhile hustle for a prospective SEO spammer. It also doesn’t hurt that generic New Age sonic compositions are relatively easy to make (or rip) compared to other types of music.
  And so a seemingly unending stream of “artists” battle in an audio turf war over various niches of faintly wellness-related audio. These profiles often have dozens to hundreds of albums, each saturated with keywords. Individual pages using this method regularly rack up tens to hundreds of thousands of monthly plays. Some daisy-chain featured artists in their metadata to populate multiple pages with slightly different names, while others simply reupload the same exact tracks over and over again in repackaged albums.
  Another tack involves bare-bones versions of popular, generic, kid-centric songs like “Happy Birthday.” In fact, the “artist”  Happy Birthday alone has uploaded hundreds upon hundreds of the same dozenish versions of the tune (done in different styles, from EDM to acoustic), racking up more than 140,000 monthly listeners. Another account,  Children’s Music, is up to nearly 300,000 monthly listeners by sharing misspelled classics like “ Wheel on the Bus” and “ If Your Happy and You Know It” over and over again.
 Some “artist” profiles have named themselves after entire subgenres of music, some of which might not have a dedicated genre page or clearly labeled Spotify-created playlist. “ Lofi Chillhop” and “ LoFi Chill” (both verified artists) seem to be competing for fans of a certain type of instrumental music that  experienced a recent rebirth thanks in part to a meme, hitting more than 100,000 and 40,000 monthly listeners, respectively, by uploading beats of unknown origin. The  verified artist profile “Uk Drill” took advantage of rising interest in the British hip-hop scene by simply ripping and reposting tracks from up-and-coming MCs, reaching more than 80,000 monthly listeners. And whoever is behind the  account “Witch House” (a spooky mid-aughts style of electronic music) accumulated a trickle of traffic by uploading generic instrumentals and jamming the names of popular EDM songs, albums, and artists into the tracks’ metadata. Witch House’s music itself isn’t even witch house, but rather intentionally mislabeled tracks ripped off from places like  obscure instrumental sampler albums.
  There isn’t really an incentive for Spotify to clean up repetitive tracks or bizarre artist names. In a way, these prolific posters are just executing an extreme version of Spotify CEO Daniel Ek’s  vision of a world where artists spend less time on their music and focus more on volume. The platform’s own goal, as  Liz Pelly wrote in  The Baffler in 2017, is (arguably) to “fit music snugly into an emotional regulation capsule optimized for maximum clicks.”
 Cerf, for instance, views what he’s doing as a useful utilitarian tool. “People  want to be able to search for music not just by artist name, but also by keyword,” he said. “They want to say, ‘Oh, I’m sleeping, or I’m driving, or I’m meditating, or I have a kid and he needs to sleep, or I just need background music.’”
 Indeed, a fundamental building block of Spotify’s algorithm is to simply recognize when a listener successfully finds what they were looking for, not whether or not the music was “quality” per se. As a 2017   Vulture article points out, these artists “are providing people exactly what they want. It just so happens that what they want is ephemeral nonsense.”
 Still, some music in this vein is downright deceptive, either of noticeably low quality or having little to no connection to the given artist title.
 Tools like digital music distributor and Spotify partner DistroKid’s  DistroLock audio fingerprint technology and Spotify’s own  blockchain startup acquisitions could in theory curb some of this spam by, say, limiting reuploads of the same tracks. But these kinds of checks aren’t yet baked into Spotify’s platform.
 Increased human moderation of Spotify’s library could offer another solution, but this presents a logistical problem because of the sheer scope of the platform’s catalog. (The platform says it adds  tens of thousands of songs to its catalog every day.)
 Spotify’s editorial team functionally runs interference on certain terms people frequently game by essentially using the same strategy as the spammers, creating official pages for certain “Genres & Moods” of music, each featuring handpicked playlists within these categorizations. However, it’s  unclear who on Spotify’s editorial team makes these pages, what process they use to create them, and whether their explicit purpose is to get out in front of spam. These official Spotify pages and playlists  sometimes surface high in search, but they often have a lot of competition from artists named with almost every imaginable variation of a set of high-value terms. Plus, spammers themselves occasionally show up in the sanctioned playlists, often joined by plenty of the less intrusive fake artists.
 Howard isn’t aggrieved by people gaming the platform to get their music heard, and he highlights that people using these strategies are simply working within a broken system.
 “The tech is agnostic,” he says. “You’d be stupid not to use [Spotify’s] system to your advantage, and so people are just gaming the system. I can speak for savvy operators in the publishing world who go, ‘I’ve got all this content. I’m going to optimize it for plays.’ And they’re doing the right thing!”
title: Spotify的UX决定如何鼓励了形容词丰富的通用艺人名称的增长，从而启用了一种针对音乐的SEO垃圾邮件
title_en: How Spotify's UX decisions have encouraged the growth of adjective-laden generic artist names, enabling a kind of SEO spam for music

美国联邦调查局(FBI)和网络安全和基础设施安全局(CISA)发布这一声明，以提高人们对试图散布有关2020年选举结果的虚假信息所构成的潜在威胁的认识。外国行为者和网络罪犯可能会创建新网站，改变现有网站，并创建或分享相应的社交媒体内容，以传播虚假信息，试图败坏选举进程的声誉，破坏人们对美国民主机构的信心。
州和地方官员通常需要几天到几周的时间来证明选举的最终结果，以确保每一张合法投票的选票都得到准确统计。由于新冠肺炎协议，邮寄选票的使用率增加，可能会让官员们在选举之夜获得不完整的结果。外国行为者和网络犯罪分子可以利用认证和宣布选举结果所需的时间，散布虚假信息，包括报道选民被压制、针对选举基础设施的网络攻击、选民或选票欺诈，以及其他旨在说服公众选举非法的问题。
FBI和CISA敦促美国公众对他们消费的信息来源进行批判性评估，并从州和地方选举官员等可信的来源寻找可靠和经过核实的信息。公众还应该意识到，如果外国行为者或网络犯罪分子能够成功更改与选举相关的网站，底层数据和内部系统将保持不受损害。
从州和地方选举官员等值得信赖的来源寻找信息；核实谁制作了内容；并考虑他们的意图。
通过多个可靠来源核实有关投票或选举结果问题的任何报告，并考虑在通过社交媒体或其他途径分享此类信息之前寻找其他可靠来源。
向联邦调查局报告潜在的选举犯罪行为，例如关于投票方式、时间或地点的虚假信息。
如果合适，利用社交媒体公司提供的平台内工具来报告可疑帖子，这些帖子似乎在传播关于选举相关问题或结果的虚假或不一致的信息。
联邦调查局负责调查针对选举基础设施和其他美国民主机构的恶意外国影响行动和恶意网络活动。CISA负责保护国家的关键基础设施免受物理和网络威胁。FBI和CISA提供服务和信息，以维护美国选举过程的安全性、完整性和弹性。
联邦调查局鼓励受害者向当地外地办事处报告有关可疑或犯罪活动的信息(www.fbi.gov/contact-us/field)。有关其他帮助和最佳实践以及通用术语，请访问以下网站：
note_en:
The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) are issuing this announcement to raise awareness of the potential threat posed by attempts to spread disinformation regarding the results of the 2020 elections. Foreign actors and cybercriminals could create new websites, change existing websites, and create or share corresponding social media content to spread false information in an attempt to discredit the electoral process and undermine confidence in U.S. democratic institutions.
  State and local officials typically require several days to weeks to certify elections’ final results in order to ensure every legally cast vote is accurately counted. The increased use of mail-in ballots due to COVID-19 protocols could leave officials with incomplete results on election night. Foreign actors and cybercriminals could exploit the time required to certify and announce elections’ results by disseminating disinformation that includes reports of voter suppression, cyberattacks targeting election infrastructure, voter or ballot fraud, and other problems intended to convince the public of the elections’ illegitimacy.
  The FBI and CISA urge the American public to critically evaluate the sources of the information they consume and to seek out reliable and verified information from trusted sources, such as state and local election officials. The public should also be aware that if foreign actors or cyber criminals were able to successfully change an election-related website, the underlying data and internal systems would remain uncompromised.
  Seek out information from trustworthy sources, such as state and local election officials; verify who produced the content; and consider their intent.
  Verify through multiple reliable sources any reports about problems in voting or election results, and consider searching for other reliable sources before sharing such information via social media or other avenues.
    Report potential election crimes—such as disinformation about the manner, time, or place of voting—to the FBI.
  If appropriate, make use of in-platform tools offered by social media companies for reporting suspicious posts that appear to be spreading false or inconsistent information about election-related problems or results.
  The FBI is responsible for investigating malign foreign influence operations and malicious cyber activity targeting election infrastructure and other U.S. democratic institutions. CISA is responsible for protecting the nation’s critical infrastructure from physical and cyber threats. The FBI and CISA provide services and information to uphold the security, integrity, and resiliency of the U.S. electoral processes.
  The FBI encourages victims to report information concerning suspicious or criminal activity to their local field office ( www.fbi.gov/contact-us/field). For additional assistance and best practices, and common terms, please visit the following websites:
title: 外国演员可能散布关于2020年选举结果的虚假信息
title_en: Foreign Actors Likely to Spread Disinformation Regarding 2020 Election Results

如果巴西城市马瑙斯是一个答案，这意味着大约三分之二的人口可能被感染，500人中可能有1人在疫情结束前死亡。
5月期间，随着病毒在亚马逊州赤道首府马瑙斯迅速传播，可怕的报道描述了不堪重负的医院和新挖的坟墓。对棺材的需求是前一年的四到五倍。但自从四个月前达到峰值以来，这座拥有180万人口的城市的新冠状病毒病例和死亡人数经历了快速且无法解释的下降。
现在，来自巴西和英国的一组研究人员表示，他们知道原因--如此多的人被感染，以至于病毒的宿主即将耗尽。
圣保罗大学热带医学研究所(Institute of Tropical Medicine at the University of São Paulo)的埃斯特·萨比诺(Ester Sabino)领导的一个小组在发布给预印服务器MedRxiv的一份报告中表示，他们对银行的血液进行了病毒抗体测试，估计自从马瑙斯3月份发现第一例病例以来，44%到66%的马瑙斯人口已经感染了病毒。
萨比诺在一次电话采访中说：“从我们了解到的情况来看，这可能是世界上患病率最高的。”“死亡人数下降得非常快，我们要说的是，这是相关的。”
在美国，唐纳德·特朗普总统因为说病毒“会自行消失”而招致嘲笑。他的评论可能是指这样一个事实，即如果有足够多的人感染一种病毒，并开发出抗体来击退它，那么所谓的群体免疫力就开始在人群中建立起来：随着更多的人获得免疫力，病毒感染新的人并继续传播变得更加困难。
作者认为，这正是马瑙斯正在发生的事情。他们写道：“尽管非药物干预，加上人群行为的改变，可能有助于限制SARS-CoV-2在马瑙斯的传播，但异常高的感染率表明，群体免疫在决定疫情规模方面发挥了重要作用。”
亚马逊地区经历了病毒最严重的时期，人们在家中死亡，土著群体受到感染。然而，到了8月中旬，“华盛顿邮报”(Washington Post)记录了马瑙斯的突然转机。根据该市卫生部门的数据，该市的死亡率从5月份的一天79人的峰值下降到了9月份的每天2到3人。
目前尚不清楚病毒为什么在马瑙斯传播得如此迅速，那里的移动数据显示，人们确实在3月份开始了社交距离。Sabino和她的同事认为，密集的住房，糟糕的供水，以及作为当地交通工具的船只上的拥挤，可能加速了疫情的爆发。
根据作者的说法，马瑙斯的感染死亡率约为0.28%，或者说每350名感染病毒的人中就有一人死亡。考虑到马瑙斯并不是每个人都感染了这种病毒，全市新冠肺炎的死亡率将在五百到八百人中有一人死亡。
山上的免疫学家弗洛里安·克拉默(Florian Krammer)说。纽约西奈医院(Sinai Hospital)表示，预计一些地区将达到足以中断当地疫情爆发的免疫力水平，但这样的事件应该被认为是公共卫生的失败，而不是成功。
Krammer在推特上写道：“通过自然感染进行社区免疫不是一种战略，这是一个迹象，表明政府未能控制疫情爆发，并正在为失去的生命买单。”
其他城市应该谨慎地从马瑙斯得出结论，因为在其他因素中，马瑙斯的人口相当年轻。根据巴西地理和统计研究所的数据，那里60岁以上的公民中只有6%。在纽约市，这一数字约为16%，而对美国整体而言，这一数字为20%。老年人如果感染了这种病毒，他们死亡的风险比年轻人高得多。
巴西的数据确实表明，随着病毒的传播，一个地方可能会有多少人受到感染-这是一个被称为攻击率的概念。如果三分之二的美国人口被感染，这种病毒很容易夺走50多万美国人的生命，其中大部分是老年人。这与早期对最坏情况的预测以及最近发生的地面事件是一致的。今天，美国打破了20多万人死于埃博拉病毒的严峻纪录。每天仍有数以万计的人被感染。
在巴西，萨比诺的团队处于有利地位，可以研究大流行的轨迹，因为该小组之前参与了检查献血是否有传染性病原体。由于巴西血库保留了捐献血液的样本，他们能够回到过去，在几个时间点寻找冠状病毒抗体-这是一种被称为连续采样的技术。
萨比诺说：“很少有人有能力进行连续抽样，但在巴西，保存样本是强制性的，所以我们可以这样做。”在6月份，新献血者中冠状病毒抗体阳性的比例高达40%，尽管自那以后这个数字有所下降，因为抗体往往会随着时间的推移而减弱。
斯特拉斯克莱德大学(University Of Strathclyde)的数学建模专家加布里埃拉·戈麦斯(Gabriela Gomes)表示，新报告发现，马瑙斯患有冠状病毒抗体的人数是之前一项研究显示的两倍；免疫学家可能会继续讨论哪个发现更准确。萨比诺说，她的团队使用雅培实验室开发的一种改进的抗体测试进行分析，她说这种测试比早期研究使用的测试更敏感，遗漏的病例更少。
展望未来，亚马逊首都现在可以帮助公共卫生官员更好地了解对新冠肺炎的免疫力持续多长时间，以及病毒重新感染人们的频率。血液调查清楚地表明，随着时间的推移，人们的抗体变得更难检测到。这可能意味着个人对病毒的免疫力不是永久性的。作者在他们的预印本中写道：“马瑙斯可能会作为一个哨兵来决定人群免疫的寿命和再感染的频率。”
note_en:
If the Brazilian city of Manaus is any answer, it means about two-thirds of the population could get infected and one person in 500 could die before the epidemic winds down.
  During May, as the virus spread rapidly in Manaus, the equatorial capital of the state of Amazonas, dire reports described overwhelmed hospitals and freshly dug graves. Demand for coffins ran at four to five times figures for the previous year. But since hitting a peak four months ago, new coronavirus cases and deaths in the city of 1.8 million have undergone a rapid and unexplained decline.
  Now a group of researchers from Brazil and the United Kingdom say they know why—so many people got infected that the virus is running out of hosts.
  In  a report posted to the preprint server medRxiv, a group led by Ester Sabino, of the Institute of Tropical Medicine at the University of São Paulo, says it tested banked blood for antibodies to the virus and estimates that between 44 and 66% of the population of Manaus has been infected since the city detected its first case in March.
  “From what we learned this is probably the highest prevalence in the world,” Sabino said in a phone interview. “Deaths have dropped very rapidly, and what we’re saying is that it’s related.”
  In the US, President Donald Trump has attracted ridicule for saying the virus  “will go away” on its own. His comments may be a reference to the fact that if enough people get infected by a virus and develop antibodies to fight it off, so-called  herd immunity starts to build in the population: As more people gain immunity, it becomes harder for the virus to infect new people and continue its spread.
  That’s exactly what’s happening in Manaus, the authors believe. “Although nonpharmaceutical interventions, plus a change in population behavior, may have helped to limit SARS-CoV-2 transmission in Manaus, the unusually high infection rate suggests that herd immunity played a significant role in determining the size of the epidemic,” they wrote.
  The Amazon region has seen  the virus at its worst, with people dying at home and infections hitting indigenous groups. However, by mid-August, the Washington Post was  documenting a sudden turnaround in Manaus. From a peak of 79 deaths on one day in May, the rate in the city is down to two or three a day in September, according to its health department.
  It remains unclear why the virus spread so quickly in Manaus, where mobility data shows people did begin social distancing in March. Sabino and her colleagues think the outbreak may have been accelerated by dense housing, poor water supplies, and crowding on boats that serve as local transportation.
  According to the authors, the infection fatality rate in Manaus was about 0.28%, or one death in every 350 people infected by the virus. Considering that not everyone has caught the virus in Manaus, the city-wide covid-19 death rate would be between one in 500 and one in 800 people overall.
  Florian Krammer, an immunologist at Mt. Sinai Hospital in New York, says it was expected that some regions would reach levels of immunity high enough to interrupt local outbreaks, but that such events should be considered public health failures, not successes.
  “Community immunity via natural infection is not a strategy, it’s a sign that government failed to control an outbreak and is paying for that in lives lost,” Krammer tweeted.
  Other cities should be cautious about drawing conclusions from Manaus since, among other factors, it has a fairly young population. Just 6% of citizens there are over 60, according to Brazil’s Institute of Geography and Statistics. In New York City, that figure is around 16% and for the US overall, it’s 20%. Elderly people have a much, much higher risk of dying if they catch the virus than younger people.
  The Brazilian figures do suggest just how many people in one place could get infected as the virus spreads—a concept known as the attack rate. Were two-thirds of the US population to be infected, the virus could easily claim more than 500,000 American lives, mostly among the elderly. That’s in line with early projections for  worst-case scenarios and with recent events on the ground. The US  today surpassed the grim record of more than 200,000 deaths attributed to the virus. Tens of thousands of people are still being infected daily.
  In Brazil, Sabino’s team was well placed to study the trajectory of the pandemic because the group was previously involved with checking blood donations for transmissible pathogens. Since Brazilian blood banks retain samples of donated blood, they were able to go back and look for coronavirus antibodies at several points in time—a technique known as serial sampling.
  “Very few people have the capacity to do serial sampling, but in Brazil it’s mandatory to save samples, so we could,” says Sabino. During the month of June, a high of 40% of new blood donors were positive for coronavirus antibodies, though the number has decreased since then as antibodies tend to wane over time.
  Gabriela Gomes, a mathematical modeler at the University of Strathclyde, says the new report finds that twice as many people in Manaus had coronavirus antibodies as  a previous study had suggested; there could be ongoing discussion among immunologists over which finding is more accurate. Sabino says her team used an improved antibody test developed by Abbott Laboratories for their analysis, which she says is more sensitive than the test used for the earlier study and misses fewer cases.
  Going forward, the Amazonian capital could now help public health officials better understand  how long immunity to covid-19 lasts and how often the virus reinfects people. The blood survey clearly showed that with time, people’s antibodies become harder to detect. That could mean individual immunity to the virus is not permanent. “Manaus may act as a sentinel to determine the longevity of population immunity and frequency of reinfections,” the authors wrote in their preprint.
title: 科维德横冲直撞的巴西城市可能是世界其他地方的哨兵
title_en: City in Brazil where Covid ran amok may be a sentinel for the rest of the world

note:
周二，美国新冠肺炎死亡人数超过20万人，每天的新增病例报告仍徘徊在40,000人左右，每天的死亡人数都在700多人左右。
20万人死亡的可怕里程碑相当于66天来每天发生的9/11袭击的死亡人数。这也相当于损失了犹他州盐湖城的全部人口，或者几乎相当于纽约州罗切斯特的人口。新冠肺炎在美国的死亡人数超过了最近五次战争(朝鲜战争、越南战争、伊拉克战争、阿富汗战争和波斯湾战争)造成的美国人死亡人数的总和。
截至周二下午三点左右，新冠肺炎的死亡人数已经达到200,541人，死亡人数超过688万人。虽然这些数字是基于州卫生当局的数据，但实际死亡人数预计要高得多。
“20万人死亡的想法真的非常发人深省，在某些方面令人震惊，”该国顶级传染病专家、国家过敏和传染病研究所所长安东尼·福奇(Anthony Fuci)博士在接受CNN采访时说。
按原始数据计算，美国是世界上死亡人数最多的国家，其次是巴西(13.7万人)和印度(近8.9万人)。按人均计算，只有五个国家的死亡率较高：秘鲁、玻利维亚、智利、西班牙和巴西。
预计死亡人数和病例将继续攀升。根据一个被广泛引用的模型，到2021年1月1日，美国的死亡人数估计将达到37.8万人-这意味着在短短三个月多一点的时间里又增加了17.8万人。
在周二接受底特律一家电视台采访时，特朗普总统吹嘘自己在应对疫情方面做了一项“令人惊叹”和“难以置信”的工作。
note_en:
The US death toll from COVID-19  topped 200,000 Tuesday as daily reports of new cases still hover around  40,000 and daily deaths are in the 700s.
 The grim milestone of 200,000 deaths is equivalent to the death toll from the 9/11 attacks occurring every day for 66 days. It’s also equivalent to losing about the entire population Salt Lake City, Utah, or nearly the population of Rochester, New York. COVID-19 has killed more in the United States than the number of Americans who died in the  five most recent wars combined (the Korean War, the Vietnam War, the Iraq War, the War in Afghanistan, and the Persian Gulf War).
 By mid-afternoon Tuesday, the COVID-19 death toll had already reached 200,541 deaths, stemming from more than 6.88 million cases. While these figures are based on data from state health authorities, the actual death toll is expected to be much higher.
 “The idea of 200,000 deaths is really very sobering, in some respects stunning,” Dr. Anthony Fauci, the country’s top infectious-disease expert and director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, said on CNN.
 By raw numbers, the US has the highest death toll in the world, followed by Brazil with 137,000 deaths and India with nearly 89,000. Per capita,  only five countries have higher death rates: Peru, Bolivia, Chile, Spain, and Brazil.
 And deaths and cases are expected to continue climbing. According to a widely cited model, the US death toll is estimated to reach  378,000 by January 1, 2021—that’s an additional 178,000 in just a little over three months’ time.
 In an interview with a Detroit TV station Tuesday, President Trump boasted of doing an “amazing” and “incredible” job of handling the pandemic.
title: 美国已有超过200,000人死于新冠肺炎，未来还会有更多的人死亡
title_en: US has topped 200,000 COVID-19 deaths—and many more to come

该公司首席执行官安德鲁·帕克(Andrew Parker)表示，该公司的服务已经在17个州提供，Papa将在未来几个月内扩展到另外4个州。
帕克在Facebook上找到了一个可以作为他在佛罗里达州的祖父的朋友的人，之后启动了这项业务。
在意识到全州老年居民对陪伴和帮助的需求不同于通常由照顾者提供的那种面对面护理后，帕克推出了这项服务。Papa的员工提供的陪伴类型从帮助处理日常事务-包括交通、简单的家务活、为健康福利和医生预约提供建议，以及杂货送货-到只是交谈。
帕克说，随着对新冠肺炎疫情的反应带来的社会孤立，该公司提供这项服务的理由甚至更多。根据该公司提供的统计数据，大约一半的成年人认为自己孤独，社会孤立会使死亡风险增加29%。
帕克在一份声明中说：“我们创建Papa的唯一目标是在整个老龄化过程中支持老年人和他们的家人。”“不幸的是，新冠肺炎疫情只会加剧导致孤独和孤立的环境，我们很荣幸能够在这段困难时期提供解决方案来帮助家庭。”
根据帕克的说法，爸爸的朋友经过了严格的审查程序，所有申请者中只有8%左右成为了朋友。
这些朋友的时薪约为每小时15美元，并有机会获得奖金和其他激励措施，现在可以与与他们匹配的老年人进行虚拟和面对面的会议。
帕克说：“我们每个月大约有2万名潜在的爸爸朋友申请。”在公司早期，它只接受大学生作为朋友工作，但现在公司正在接受更广泛的潜在员工，助手年龄从18岁到45岁不等。帕克说，他们的平均年龄是29岁。
Papa监控和管理公司员工和他们的主管之间的所有虚拟互动，指出在讨论中可能会提出的问题，比如抑郁症和获得食物或药物的潜在问题。帕克说，监测的目的是确保公司的费用可以获得膳食计划、治疗师或药物。
由于新的主要投资者康卡斯特风险投资公司(Comcast Ventures)和其他支持者-包括Canaan、Initialized Capital、Sound Ventures、Pivotal Ventures、Flatiron Health的创始人和他们的投资集团运营商Partners，以及Behance的创始人斯科特·贝尔斯基-Papa现在又有了1800万美元的融资可以合作-Papa专注于开发新产品和扩大服务范围。
到目前为止，该公司已经筹集了3100万美元，预计到2021年1月将在所有50个州运营。该公司的同伴服务可通过健康计划向会员提供，并作为雇主福利提供。
康卡斯特风险投资公司(Comcast Ventures)负责人法蒂玛·侯赛因(Fatima Husain)在一份声明中表示：“Papa正在帮助越来越多的美国老年人在家养老，同时降低医疗计划的护理成本，并为同伴护理专业人员创造有意义的就业机会。”“
note_en:
The company’s services are already available in 17 states and Papa is going to expand to another four states in the next few months, according to chief executive Andrew Parker.
 Parker launched the business after reaching out on Facebook to find someone who could serve as a pal for his own grandfather in Florida.
  After realizing that there was a need among elderly residents across the state for companionship and assistance that differed from the kind of in-person care that would typically be provided by a caregiver, Parker launched the service. The kinds of companionship Papa’s employees offer range from helping with everyday tasks — including transportation, light household chores, advising with health benefits and doctor’s appointments, and grocery delivery — to just conversation.
 With the social isolation brought on by responses to the COVID-19 pandemic there are even more reasons for the company’s service, Parker said. Roughly half of adults consider themselves lonely, and social isolation increases the risk of death by 29%, according to statistics provided by the company.
 “We created Papa with the singular goal of supporting older adults and their families throughout the aging journey,” said Parker, in a statement. “The COVID-19 pandemic has unfortunately only intensified circumstances leading to loneliness and isolation, and we’re honored to be able to offer solutions to help families during this difficult time.”
 Papa’s pals go through a stringent vetting process, according to Parker, and only about 8% of all applicants become pals.
 These pals get paid an hourly rate of around $15 per hour and have the opportunity to receive bonuses and other incentives, and are now available for virtual and in-person sessions with the older adults they’re matched with.
 “We have about 20,000 potential Papa pals apply a month,” said Parker. In the company’s early days it only accepted college students to work as pals, but now the company is accepting a broader range of potential employees, with assistants ranging from 18 to 45 years old. The average age, Parker said, is 29.
 Papa monitors and manages all virtual interactions between the company’s employees and their charges, flagging issues that may be raised in discussions, like depression and potential problems getting access to food or medications. The monitoring is designed to ensure that meal plans, therapists or medication can be made available to the company’s charges, said Parker.
 Now that there’s $18 million more in financing for the company to work with, thanks to new lead investor  Comcast Ventures   and other backers — including Canaan, Initialized Capital,  Sound Ventures,   Pivotal Ventures, the founders of  Flatiron Health   and their investment group  Operator Partners,   along with Behance founder,  Scott Belsky   — Papa is focused on developing new products and expanding the scope of its services.
 The company has raised $31 million to date and expects to be operating in all 50 states by January 2021. The company’s companion services are available to members through health plans and as an employer benefit.
 “Papa is enabling a growing number of older Americans to age at home, while reducing the cost of care for health plans and creating meaningful jobs for companion care professionals,” said Fatima Husain, principal at Comcast Ventures, in a statement. “

note: 当然，互联网是一项正在进行中的工作。至少，美国在线和时代华纳的合并是对下一代互联网技术的一场赌博。目前，大多数家庭用户通过调制解调器和老式电话线提供拨号服务。取而代之的技术是宽带，通过改进电缆线路、光纤、卫星或更好的电话线，将大幅提高传输速度。联邦通信委员会说，今天的高速电话调制解调器可以在24分钟内下载10兆字节的文件(相当于10到20分钟的电影剪辑)。速度更快的电缆可以在大约一分钟内完成同样的工作。互联网、电视和电话将会融合在一起。无论如何，这是传统观点。对于美国在线-时代华纳来说，宏伟的战略是既提供传输服务(时代华纳拥有第二大有线电视系统)，又提供内容服务(从互联网服务到电影、电视节目、杂志和音乐)。其他公司也怀有类似的野心。但我们不知道这一愿景或其他一些愿景是否会实现。技术通过重塑人类状况获得了历史上的份量。古腾堡的出版引导了大众识字，促进了新教改革(通过破坏神职人员的神学垄断)，并通过轻松的信息交换，促成了科学革命。在19世纪，铁路创造了一个真正的全国性的美国市场，有利于大规模生产和消费社会。要加入这个联盟，互联网必须不仅仅是电子邮件或营销平台。如果你在网上买书或买车，交易的关键部分仍然是书或车。特别是在B2B商务中，互联网可能会通过更多的价格竞争和供应商选择来提高效率。但这些都是程度上的变化，而不是善意的。深思熟虑的互联网爱好者对其更大的意义提供了看似合理的猜测。在过去的200年里，我们建立了大规模生产的工业经济。。。时事通讯Release 1.0的编辑埃丝特·戴森(Esther Dyson)说，还有大众市场。通过提供如此多的信息，互联网使消费者能够逃离大众市场；通过使信息如此容易分发，它使人们能够成为独立的生产者。她说，最大的影响是赋予个人更多的生活权力，同时让政府、企业--任何人--更难保密经营。“连线”杂志的凯文·凯利(Kevin Kelly)是“新经济新规则”(New Rules For The New Economy)的作者，他认为互联网实际上被低估了。“新经济的新规则”(New Rules For The New Economy)一书的作者凯文·凯利(Kevin Kelly)认为，互联网实际上被低估了。在10年后上网的所有人中，今天只有十分之一的人上网。(注：根据Dataquest的数据，1999年，全球有5800万人上网。)。这可能会加速全球贸易，削弱(无论是好是坏)各国政府。所有的大问题都还没有解决。网络会促进个人自主权，还是会侵犯隐私？它会增加人们的经济独立性，还是会扩大公司权力(美国在线-时代华纳不会是俾格米人)？在答案变得清晰之前，互联网必须达到经济可行性。虽然蓬勃发展，但它现在基本上是一个资本主义慈善机构。它上面的几乎所有东西都被赠送或亏本出售。零售电子商务微不足道。1999年，它占美国消费者支出的比例不到0.5%。广告也是如此。据环球麦肯(Universal McCann)的罗伯特·科恩(Robert Coen)估计，1999年，美国广告总额为2150亿美元，其中互联网广告占18亿美元。互联网的巨大财富主要来自股票投机或基础设施建设--提供计算机、软件和光纤。据主要供应商北电网络称，1999年这一支出为3660亿美元。投资迟早要付出回报，否则就会停止。即使互联网蓬勃发展，它也可能比早期的Big Things更小。我们的历史健忘症可以从一位田纳西州农民在20世纪40年代的一次教堂会议上的话中受益。“兄弟姐妹们，我想告诉你们这件事，”他说。世界上最伟大的事情是心中有上帝的爱，其次是家里有电。互联网真的能超越这一点吗？
note_en: Of course, the Internet is a work in progress. If nothing else, the AOL-Time Warner merger is a gamble on the next generation of Internet technology. At present most home users have dial-up service through modems and old phone lines. The replacement technology is "broadband," which--through improved cable lines, fiber optics, satellites or better phone lines--would sharply raise transmission speeds. A fast phone modem today would allow a 10-megabyte file (equivalent to a 10- to 20-minute movie clip) to be downloaded in 24 minutes, says the Federal Communications Commission. Faster cable lines can do the same job in about a minute. The Internet, television and the telephone will blend. This, at any rate, is the conventional wisdom. For AOL-Time Warner, the grand strategy is to provide both transmission services (Time Warner owns the second-largest cable system) and "content" (from Internet services to films, TV programs, magazines, music). Other companies harbor similar ambitions. But we don't know whether this vision, or some other, will materialize. Technologies acquire historical weight by reshaping the human condition. Gutenberg's press led to mass literacy, fostered the Protestant Reformation (by undermining the clergy's theological monopoly) and, through the easy exchange of information, enabled the scientific revolution. In the 19th century railroads created a truly national American market that favored mass production and the consumer society. To join this league, the Internet must be more than e-mail or a marketing platform. If you buy a book or car on the Net, the critical part of the transaction is still the book or car. Especially in business-to-business commerce, the Internet may improve efficiency through more price competition and supplier choice. But these are changes of degree, not kind. Thoughtful Internet enthusiasts offer plausible speculations about its greater meaning. "Over the past 200 years, we have built up industrial economies of mass production . . . and mass markets," says Esther Dyson, editor of Release 1.0, a newsletter. By providing so much information, the Internet empowers consumers to escape mass markets; by making information so easy to dispense, it enables people to become independent producers. "The major impact is to give individuals more power over their lives," she says, while making it "tougher for governments, businesses--anyone--to operate in secrecy." Kevin Kelly of Wired magazine, author of "New Rules for the New Economy," argues that the "Internet is actually being underhyped. Of all the people [who will be] online in 10 years, only a tenth are online today." (Note: In 1999, there were 58 million worldwide connections, says Dataquest.) This could accelerate global commerce and weaken (for good or ill) national governments. All the large issues remain unsettled. Will the Net boost individual autonomy--or infringe on privacy? Will it increase people's economic independence--or expand corporate power (AOL-Time Warner would be no pygmy)? Before answers become clear, the Internet will have to attain economic viability. Though booming, it is now largely a capitalist charity. Almost everything on it is being given away or sold at a loss. Retail e-commerce is puny. In 1999 it amounted to less than 0.5 percent of U.S. consumer spending. Ditto for advertising. In 1999 Internet ads amounted to $1.8 billion out of total U.S. advertising of $215 billion, estimates Robert Coen of Universal McCann. The great Internet fortunes arise mainly from stock speculation or building the infrastructure--supplying computers, software and fiber optics. In 1999 this spending was $366 billion, says Nortel Networks, a major supplier. Sooner or later, the investment must pay a return, or it will stop. Even if the Internet flourishes, it may remain smaller than earlier Big Things. Our historical amnesia could benefit from the words of a Tennessee farmer at a church meeting in the 1940s. "Brothers and sisters, I want to tell you this," he said. "The greatest thing on earth is to have the love of God in your heart, and the next greatest is to have electricity in your home." Can the Internet really top that?
title: 古腾堡VS网(2000)

note: 在奥斯汀，弗兰尼根说，警察局的预算在过去10年里翻了一番，而人口增长了大约30%。通过剥离这个庞然大物的资金，奥斯汀能够将Covid有限的收入推向其他需求旺盛的公共产品，如紧急医疗服务、公园维护和心理健康。该市一名发言人表示，除了对警察局的裁员外，没有其他裁员。弗兰尼根说：“当然，在警察部门预算上修正这一轨迹是我们即使在这些疯狂荒谬的时期也能让这座城市继续前进的另一大原因。”他说，州一级保守派政客的强烈反对，突显了美国城市面临的另一个财政威胁。共和党州长格雷格·阿博特(Greg Abbott)威胁说，当立法机构重新开会时，他将采取行动冻结从警察部门转移资金的城市的财产税收入。“这是在德克萨斯州乃至整个美国越来越多地对城市地区不利的情况下做出的。共和党的基础主要集中在远郊和农村地区，通过损害城市的立法，在共和党煽动我们对他们的怨恨政治的选举战略和缩减公共部门的意识形态目标之间非常吻合。这可以在国家层面上看到，大多数参议院共和党人拒绝支持所需的援助，在州一级，比如阿博特和他的盟友通过的财产税上限，以防止城市在没有直接向选民提出问题的情况下提高税率超过3.5%。去年夏天，德克萨斯州众议院共和党议长丹尼斯·邦宁(Dennis Bonnen)幸灾乐祸地被录下：“我的目标是让这成为市县立法机构历史上最糟糕的一次会议。”而他的亲密盟友、当时的共和党党团会议主席、州众议员达斯汀·伯罗斯(Dustin Burrow)则表示，“我们憎恨市县。”雅培冻结“撤资警察”城市财产税的新提议得到了Bonnen的支持，在政策公布的记者会上，Bonnen坐在州长旁边.“在其他国家，每个地区都有围绕纾困的政治因素，但他们还在继续，因为他们意识到，如果你允许多个地区停滞不前，在经济上受苦，这对整个国家都不好，”布朗大学国际政治经济学教授Mark Blyth说.。“在美国，你有一个政党，它相信行政国家的退出和解散。每次发生这种情况，都是好消息(从他们的角度来看)。你在摧毁那个级别的政府。这是一个功能，而不是一个漏洞。“。这让许多美国城市陷入了两难境地，一方面是如果共和党人控制着国会的一个分支，联邦政府就不愿意或无法帮助它们；要么是州政府往往被那些似乎积极希望它们作恶的人控制着；要么是经济无情地将成果集中在少数几个地区。大多数城市都不像奥斯汀。甚至在大流行来袭之前，超过四分之一的美国市政当局预计他们的普通资金将在2020年收缩，几乎每个美国城市现在都充斥着赤字。对于那些处于富尔顿紧缩措施金字塔顶端的人--处于极端境地的政府被迫从根本上重新思考如何运作--也有噩梦般的选择。在本已处于不利地位的城市，地方当局可以被撤职或被剥夺权力，各州可以任命不受选民政治要求影响的政策制定者。市政功能可以全部取消，当路灯被切断时，将社区笼罩在黑暗中。在密歇根州弗林特，正是财政紧缩措施导致一名未经选举产生的应急经理放弃了该市的旧供水系统，改用新的水源，这剥离了管道的保护层，并用铅毒死了一代城市儿童。全国城市联盟(National League Of Cities)负责住房和经济发展的立法主任迈克尔·华莱士(Michael Wallace)表示：“如果没有援助到来，那么城市领导人将不得不做出一些非常激烈的选择。”“弗林特是一种财政问题凌驾于公共卫生之上的情况。想象一下，如果每个州都有三四种这样的情况，城市完全处于财政危险之中。当每个州手上都有弗林特时，会是什么样子？“。这种惨淡的情景不会在奥斯汀展开，奥斯汀无疑是美国为数不多的几个获胜地区之一。它受到州议会大厦(尽管公共部门的削减也会损害这座城市)、美国最大的大学之一、快速增长的科技行业以及允许餐馆和酒吧在冬天继续户外就座的气候的提振。其他人则处于重新定位的位置
note_en: In Austin, Flannigan says the police department’s budget has doubled in the last 10 years, while the population grew by roughly 30%. By peeling funds away from that behemoth, Austin was able to push Covid-limited revenues towards other public goods in high demand like emergency medical services, public park maintenance, and mental health. A city spokesperson says that there have been no other cuts beyond those to the police department. “Course correcting that trajectory on the police department budget is another big reason why we were able to keep the city moving forward even in these crazy ridiculous times,” says Flannigan.  The backlash from conservative politicians at the state level has been intense and highlights another fiscal threat facing American cities. Republican Governor Greg Abbott has threatened that when the legislature is back in session he will move to   freeze property tax revenues for cities  that shift funding from their police departments.   This comes amid a larger landscape in Texas, and America as a whole, that is increasingly stacked against urban areas. The Republican base is primarily centered in the exurbs and in rural areas, and passing legislation that harms cities dovetails nicely between the party’s electoral strategy of stoking us-versus-them resentment politics and its ideological goal of shrinking the public sector.  This can be seen at the national level in the refusal of most   Senate Republicans to countenance needed aid , and at the state level in laws like the property tax cap that Abbot and his allies passed   to prevent cities from increasing rates by more than 3.5%  without putting the question directly to voters. Last summer, the Republican speaker of the Texas House Dennis Bonnen was   caught on tape gloating : “My goal is for this to be the worst session in the history of the legislature for cities and counties,” while state representative Dustin Burrows, his close ally and then chair of the Republican caucus, was recorded saying “we hate cities and counties.” Abbott’s new proposal to freeze property taxes of cities that “defund police” is supported by Bonnen, who sat next to the governor at the press conference where the policy was unveiled.   “In other countries, there’s always politics around bailouts from region to region, but they get on with them because they realize if you allow a number of regions to stagnate and suffer economically, that’s not good for the country overall,” says Mark Blyth, a professor of international political economy at Brown University. “In America you have a political party that believes in the defenestration and dismantling of the administrative state. Every time this happens, it’s good news [from their perspective]. You’re destroying governments at that level. It’s a feature, not a bug.” That leaves many American cities stuck in a bind between a federal government that is unwilling or unable to help them if Republicans command even one branch of Congress, state governments that are often controlled by those who seem to actively wish them ill, and an economy that unrelentingly focuses its fruits upon a handful of regions. Most cities are not like Austin. Even before the pandemic hit,   over a quarter of American municipalities expected to see their general funds shrink  in 2020 and nearly every US city is now awash in red ink. For those at the top of Fulton’s pyramid of austerity measures, where governments at extremis are forced to radically rethink how they operate, there are nightmarish options too.  In already disadvantaged cities,   local authorities can be removed or disempowered , and states can appoint policymakers who are insulated from the political demands of voters. Municipal functions can be eliminated whole cloth,   swathing neighborhoods in darkness  as the street lights are cut off. In Flint, Michigan, it was fiscal austerity measures that resulted in   an unelected emergency manager abandoning the city’s old water system  and changing to a new source, which stripped the pipes of their protective coating and poisoned a generation of city children with lead.  “If there is no aid coming, then city leaders will have to make some very drastic choices,” says Michael Wallace, legislative director for housing and economic development with the National League of Cities. “Flint was a situation where fiscal concerns overrode public health. Imagine if every state had three or four situations like that, with cities in complete fiscal jeopardy. What’s it going to look like when every state has a Flint on their hands?” This kind of bleak scenario won’t unfold in Austin, which is inarguably in one of America’s handful of winning regions. It is buoyed by the state capitol (although public sector cuts at that level will hurt the city too), one of the nation’s largest universities, a rapidly growing tech sector, and a climate that allows restaurants and bars to continue outdoor seating into the winter. Others are positioned to do relatively better too. Although San Francisco is seeing signs of Covid-related flight, the larger Bay Area has not seen substantial out-migration or a real estate sell off because of the pandemic (the toll of wildfires on the highly successful metro regions of the West Coast is still unclear). The National League of Cities projects that Connecticut’s urban areas will suffer the smallest revenue shortfalls in the nation.  “Rich counties don’t suffer many recession costs, then they bounce back faster,” says Blyth, who authored    Austerity: The History of a Dangerous Idea . “Are the suburbs going to bounce back faster? Yes. Are the white middle class going to have their schools funded? Yes. Are the cities that have no tax base, that have been in trouble, that are racially divided, are they going to have even less money? Absolutely. Is that unusual? No. That’s how America works.”
title: 没有援助，几乎没有美国城市能够避免紧缩
title_en: Absent aid, few US cities will be able to avoid austerity

note:
波士顿-新冠肺炎患者的一系列症状中，包括严重的肺部炎症，导致肺功能下降，需要呼吸机，甚至可能死亡。
根据波士顿大学研究人员的最新数据，新冠肺炎是急性呼吸道感染的变种，通过攻击肺细胞和抑制身体的免疫反应，对呼吸系统造成严重破坏。
波士顿大学再生医学中心和国家新兴传染病实验室的医生利用人类干细胞开发的肺组织(最初用于研究吸烟对肺的影响)揭示了新冠肺炎用来侵蚀感染患者肺功能的过程。
该研究小组说，该病毒最初通过阻止名为干扰素的蛋白质来阻止肺细胞向免疫系统寻求帮助。
“我们的数据证实，SARS-CoV-2在感染后早期阻止细胞激活免疫系统中的一个抗病毒分支。研究作者Darrell Kotton博士解释说：“细胞通常会发出的信号，一种叫做干扰素的微小蛋白质，在疾病的威胁下会分泌出来，但却被推迟了几天，让SARS-CoV-2有足够的时间传播和杀死细胞，引发死亡细胞碎片和其他炎症的堆积，”研究作者Darrell Kotton博士解释说。
当免疫系统能够对病毒做出反应时，肺组织已经布满了被感染或死亡和濒临死亡的细胞。
这项研究的两名作者也是波士顿医学中心治疗新冠肺炎肺炎患者的肺部和重症监护医生。
“它们与我们体内的活生生的呼吸细胞不一样，但它们是最接近的。”--@Crem_Boston主管@kottond解释了与@neidl🔬一起研究模仿人类肺部的肺组织中的#covid🦠的工作。w/@bu_twets：www.crem_twets：@crem_twets：@crem_tweet：@crem_tweet：@kottond。
-波士顿医疗中心(@the_BMC)2020年9月18日。
波士顿大学(Boston University)的一份新闻稿称，CReM的成员已经开发出复杂的人类肺组织模型-从人类干细胞中生长出来的肺细胞的三维结构，称为“肺类器官”-他们已经在波士顿大学和其他地方的合作者那里使用这种模型来研究一系列慢性和急性肺部疾病。
note_en:
BOSTON —  Among a sea of symptoms in patients with COVID-19 is severe inflammation of the lungs, leading to decreased lung function, the need for a ventilator and possibly even death.
  COVID-19, a variation of acute respiratory infections, wreaks havoc on the respiratory system by attacking lung cells and suppressing the body’s immune response, according to new data from Boston University researchers.
  Using lung tissue developed by human stem cells, which were originally used to study the effects of smoking on the lungs, doctors at the  Boston University Center for Regenerative Medicine (CReM) and  National Emerging Infections Diseases Laboratories (NEIDL) have uncovered the process that COVID-19 uses to erode lung function in infected patients.
  The virus initially prevents the lung cells from calling for help from the immune system by stopping proteins called interferons, the team said.
  “Our data confirms that SARS-CoV-2 blocks cells from activating one of the anti-viral branches of the immune system early on after infection has set in. The signal the cells would typically send out, a tiny protein called interferon that they exude under threat of disease, are instead delayed for several days, giving SARS-CoV-2 plenty of time to spread and kill cells, triggering a buildup of dead cell debris and other inflammation,” explains study author Dr. Darrell Kotton.
  By the time the immune system can respond to the virus, the lung tissue is already riddled with infected or dead and dying cells.
    Two of the study’s authors are also pulmonary and critical care physicians taking care of patients with COVID-19 pneumonia at Boston Medical Center
  “They’re not identical to the living, breathing cells inside our bodies, but they are the closest thing to it,”-  @CReM_Boston director  @kottond explains the work being done w/  @neidl 🔬 to study  #COVID 🦠 in lung tissue mimicking human lungs w/  @BU_Tweets:  https://t.co/23L5ZLqZkT
— Boston Medical Center (@The_BMC)  September 18, 2020
  "Members of the CReM have developed sophisticated models of human lung tissue—three-dimensional structures of lung cells, called “lung organoids,” grown from human stem cells—which they’ve used at BU and with collaborators elsewhere to study a range of chronic and acute lung diseases," a Boston University release stated.
title: 北卡罗来纳大学的研究人员说，他们发现了导致新冠肺炎致命肺部炎症的原因
title_en: BU researchers say they found what causes fatal lung inflammation in Covid-19
